---
title: "How Combat Works"
description: "The probabilistic, per-soldier model behind every clash --- hits, wounds, stamina, footing, and the spear wall."
---

Battles in Sparta are not resolved by two numbers trading blows. Every soldier
fights the individual in front of them, carries their own health, stamina, and
footing, and each blow is rolled on its own. The tactics you know --- flanking
kills faster, never turn your back, spears screen, a braced line stops a charge,
a surrounded man tires and falls --- are not bonuses bolted on. They **emerge**
from where bodies are and the dice each strike rolls. This page lays out those dice.

## What each soldier carries

Every soldier has live state that combat reads and changes moment to moment:

- **Health** --- wounds add up here; at zero, the soldier falls.
- **Stamina** --- spent by every action; when it runs low, everything gets worse.
- **Facing** --- the direction they are turned. You can only defend what you face.
- **Stance** --- from *at ease*, through *advancing* and *sprinting*, to *braced*
  (set to receive a hit), or knocked **prone**. Stance sets how hard you hit on the
  move, how well you hold your ground, and how fast you tire --- and changing it
  takes time, so you must be set *before* the blow arrives.

and fixed traits from their regiment:

- **Skill** $s$ --- training. Lands blows, parries, beats shields, tires slower.
- **Armour** $a$ --- soaks part of a landed blow, from *any* angle.
- **Shield** $b$ --- blocks blows, but only ones you face.
- **Lethality** $\ell$ --- how deadly the weapon is.
- **Reach** $r$ --- how far the weapon strikes (a spear out-reaches a sword).
- **Mass** $m$ --- how hard they are to shove or knock down (a horse is heavy).

Two things scale *everything* a soldier does: being **wounded** and being
**winded**. A hurt or exhausted soldier hits softer, defends weaker, and is easier
to put down --- so wounds and fatigue snowball.

## One blow, step by step

When a soldier swings at the enemy in front of them, the game resolves a closing
speed, a contest, a wound, a stamina cost, and a shove.

**0. How fast are they closing?** The attacker's speed *toward* the target --- call
it the **charge** $c$ --- is zero standing still and large for a galloping horse. It
makes the blow harder to dodge, harder-hitting, and harder-shoving, all at once.
Closing speed belongs to **both** fighters: it counts for *whoever strikes during
the rush*, in either direction, and head-on speeds **add**. So a spearman who holds
his point into a charge spits the rider on its own momentum, and two horsemen
charging each other trade blows at *combined* speed --- cavalry into cavalry is the
deadliest clash there is.

**1. Does it land? (attack vs. defence)** The blow is an *opposed* roll: the
attacker's offence against the defender's **active defence** --- footwork, parry,
shield. Crucially, you can only actively defend what you can **see**:

$$p_{\mathrm{land}} = L\big(\beta\,(\mathcal{A} - \mathcal{D})\big),
\qquad
\mathcal{D} = \phi\,(s_D + \lambda\,b_D)\cdots$$

where $\phi$ is your **facing** against the incoming blow: full to the front, little
to the flank, **zero to the back**. A blow to a soldier's back meets no parry and no
shield --- only armour. Skill, shields, and footwork all live on the front. Wound
the attacker or wind the defender and these scores shift; nothing is ever a
guaranteed hit or miss.

**2. Does it wound?** A blow that lands subtracts from the defender's **health** ---
we don't flip a kill-or-not coin, we track damage. Its size is the weapon's
lethality, boosted by the charge and blunted by armour:

$$\Delta h = D_0\,\ell_A\,(1 + c)\,(1 - a_D)\cdots$$

There is no separate "injured" flag: health just drops, and a soldier near death
fights weaker, so the next wound comes easier than the last. At zero health they
fall. Armour is the only thing that protects a man hit from behind.

**3. Everyone tires.** Stamina is spent by **attacking**, by **defending** (every
blow you meet costs you --- a charge costs more), and by **getting up** after being
knocked down. It refills only while resting. So a soldier under sustained pressure,
or surrounded, burns through stamina, their defence collapses, and then blows land
freely. Fatigue, not a hidden timer, is what breaks a line.

**4. The shove.** Every blow that connects --- even a blocked one --- shoves the
target back, harder from a charge and less against a heavy soldier:

$$J = J_0\,\frac{\ell_A\,(1 + c)}{m_D}\cdots$$

A blocked blow draws no blood but still pushes --- which is how a spear wall drives
a stalled enemy backward.

## Knocked down --- and the domino

A hard enough shove **knocks a soldier prone**. Bracing and mass make you harder to
topple; once down, you defend nothing, can't strike, and must spend time *and*
stamina scrambling up --- which is exactly when the next blow kills you.

A shoved soldier also collides with whoever is behind them. Bracing soaks only so
much --- but the men behind help. **Braced comrades in a tight file back up the man
in front**, so the front rank resists with the *whole column's* footing, not one
man's. A shove smaller than that combined footing dies at the front; a hit
**stronger** than the column can absorb bowls the front man over and the surplus
dominoes rearward, fading as it goes:

$$J_{i+1} = \tau\,\big(J_i - \mathrm{capacity}_i\big)_+, \qquad 0 < \tau < 1.$$

So a light shove dies at the first set rank; a heavy charge punches *through*,
toppling rank after rank until it is spent --- and the **deeper** the braced file,
the bigger the hit it takes to break through. You are more braced when **set** (the
braced stance), in **tight formation**, and **facing into** the blow --- the same
facing that lets you defend, and the support vanishes the instant the file loosens,
opens a gap, or is hit from a side it isn't facing. A loose, flanked, or sprinting
file dominoes and goes down; a deep, set, front-facing shield wall stands.

## Why your tactics work

Everything tactical comes out of the rolls above:

- **Flank and surround.** A soldier hemmed in is in reach of more enemies (more
  blows rolled at them), burns stamina meeting all of them (their defence
  collapses), and takes hits they **can't face** (armour only). Three effects, one
  cause --- not a "rear bonus" number. Get around the flank and the line melts.
- **Never show your back.** Facing gates both your defence and your footing. A
  soldier fighting to their front is hard to hit and hard to topple; the moment they
  turn --- routing, or caught from behind --- defence and footing vanish and they're
  run down. That's why a breaking line dies so fast.
- **Spears screen.** A spear's reach holds shorter weapons out past their own reach:
  the spearmen jab and shove while the swordsmen can't answer. Put a spear line in
  front of what you want to protect.
- **Brace for the charge --- in time.** A charge lights up everything at once ---
  harder to dodge, deadlier, and a shove that bowls men over and lays them out. Into
  a loose line it's a massacre. A **braced, deep** spear line, set and facing, soaks
  it at the front rank --- the ranks behind buttressing the front --- stays on its
  feet, and out-reaches the horse: the charge breaks on the hedge. But setting the
  brace takes time, so order it *before* contact (a line caught still at ease is
  ridden down), keep it **deep** (a shallow braced line can be punched through), and
  a heavy enough charge topples men even when braced. Never take a charge loose or in
  the flank; never send horse into set spears.
- **Armour and shields buy time.** Heavy, shielded troops turn wounds into blocked
  shoves and glancing hits --- they grind slowly and endure. Light troops caught in
  the press evaporate.
- **Fight fresh, fight skilled.** Skill and stamina help at *both* ends --- landing
  more, defending more, tiring slower. Rested veterans beat winded levies by more
  than the headcount suggests, so pace your commitment.

::: {.callout-note}
The exact constants ($\beta$, $D_0$, $\tau$, the stamina costs, the knockdown
threshold, and the rest) are balance dials we tune against playtests; the **shape**
of the model above is what's fixed. For the full specification, see the
`docs/combat-model.md` design note in the project repository.
:::
